title: The Side Effects of Linoleic Acid on Your Thyroid

chunks.title: Impact of Seed Oils on Metabolism
chunks.summary: The segment discusses the effects of polyunsaturated fatty acids, specifically from seed oils, on human metabolism and thyroid function. It highlights how these fatty acids can interfere with thyroid hormone production, release, and binding, leading to potential health issues.
chunks.transcript: And I'm curious how you think seed oils might just overall be affecting the metabolism or do they have any unique localized effects on thyroid harms? >> Yeah, I mean again this is there's Ray Pete has published a lot of interesting research here on on polyunsaturated fatty acids in general. Even more broad than seed oils. Seed oils are omega-6 rich linoleic acid. But you know polyunsaturated fatty acids in general are potentially stressful for the human body. The more double bonds a molecule has the more of an oxidative load it has. So there's evidence that at almost every level of thyroid hormone production, release and binding, polyunsaturated fatty acids can interfere. They can interfere with the production of thyroid hormones. They can interfere with the release of thyroid hormones. They can displace thyroid hormones and vitamin A from trans thyroid, which is one of the carrier proteins for thyroid hormones. And they can potentially interfere with T3 binding to its receptor within the cells or at a cellular level. So at every level, polyunsaturated fatty acids are potentially problematic in the mitochondria. I think that increased incorporation of polyunsaturated fatty acids into the membranes creates an oxidative stress and your body is just managing a a a leaky membrane. And so you have to think that double it's just basic organic chemistry, right? We need some balance of of saturated and unsaturated poly. We need some balance of saturated and unsaturated fatty acids for membranes to attain the proper fluidity. But if you if you eat more polyunsaturated fatty acids, your membranes will contain more polyunsaturated fatty acids. This is very important to understand. The human body can make saturated fat and interconvert that with monounsaturated fat. But we don't make polyunsaturated fat. So we store what we eat. So the composition of our fat cells, of our membranes, of our brain is directly proportional to the amount of polyunsaturated fatty acids we eat. And more is not better. Historically, ancestrally polyunsaturated fatty acids were in short supply. We don't have seed oils before a 100 years ago. And nuts are very, very rare, seasonal, and very hard to obtain. And so consumption of polyunsaturated fatty acids by indigenous cultures is 1 to 2% max of their calories. Mhm. >> Today we're upwards of 10 to 15%. You know, we're 10 plus X on the polyunsaturated fatty acids. And I think there's an evolutionary mismatch here that's creating problems for humans. We live in this interesting world today where we get to be in houses and I get to talk to you via photons and electrons and the Ethernet and satellites. But we have to understand that our bodies still expect us to be living the same way our ancestors did 75,000 years ago. And just because certain things are in more supply today, polyunsaturated fatty acids being the current topic of conversation, especially in junk foods, but also in the feed of animals we're eating, uh, doesn't mean that it's okay for us. And if we do things that are evolutionarily discordant, we run into problems as humans. And I think this is the main issue with polyunsaturated

chunks.title: The Evolutionary Mismatch of Polyunsaturated Fats
chunks.summary: The speaker explains the historical context of polyunsaturated fatty acid consumption in human diets. They emphasize that modern diets contain significantly more of these fats than ancestral diets, creating an evolutionary mismatch that may contribute to health problems.

chunks.title: Concerns About Fish Oil Supplementation
chunks.summary: This segment explores the potential risks of excessive omega-3 fatty acid supplementation from fish oil. The speaker argues that while mild supplementation can be beneficial, high doses may lead to health issues such as increased chances of heart arrhythmias.
chunks.transcript: evidence that at doses greater than four grams a day, these omega-3 fatty acids in fish oils increase the incidence of atrial fibrillation, which is a heart arhythmia. I think that's just the tip of the iceberg. So, these these um these urgings, you know, from health influencers to just increase your omega-3s over and over up and up and up doesn't make a lot of sense to me. I really disagree with this. I think that for most people, if you are eating egg yolks and you are eating grass-fed meat, you are getting all the omega-3s you need. There's absolutely no need to supplement with a fish oil. chunks.title: Risks Associated with Fish Consumption
chunks.summary: The discussion shifts to the risks posed by consuming fish due to contamination with heavy metals and microplastics. The speaker warns that regular fish consumption can lead to elevated levels of harmful substances in the body, outweighing potential health benefits.
